speaker: interviewer
text: You had no false names. The Russians liberated you when? '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: January 25, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a place called Jachcice.

speaker: interviewer
text: J?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-G-A-D-S-C-H-U-T-Z. But I won't swear for the spelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is in?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 50 kilometers from Breslau, which is Wroclaw. Breslau, Poland. It was Eastern Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I sit down? Yes? The date is April 3, 1995. We're interviewing Isabella Leitner. My name is Raquel Grunwald. We're in New York City, USA. And the language we'll be using is English. The glasses, OK? Hi. Thank you very much for letting us into your home. Could you give us your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isabella Leitner, L-E-I-T-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name before you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isabella Katz, K-A-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us the town you're from? And if you would spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kisvarda, K-I-S-V-A-R-D-A, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- did you have a Hebrew name or a Yiddish name in Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bela.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bela? And that's what they called you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they called me [? Bybee. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Bybee? ?] So that was sort of a term of endearment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a large family? Sisters? Brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had five-- there were five girls and one boy. And originally, there were-- some years before that, there were twin brothers that I had, who were eight months old when they died. And they caught whooping cough, I believe, from the nurse. And then they were taken to the best hospital in Hungary, in Debrecen. And my mother nearly went crazy when they both died and claimed that there was anti-Semitism then involved in the treatment of the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: My god.

speaker: interviewee
text: That goes back many, many years. I was-- oh, I suppose 60-some years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Twin boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have always heard about it from my mother that, that there was anti-Semitism in the medical--

speaker: interviewer
text: The treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: --treatment of the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of the siblings that are alive, were alive before the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zippy was my oldest sister. The next one is Chica. I am giving you the names that are-- that we used. They were not-- you know, they had Yiddish names, and they had Hungarian names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, then after Chica, it's myself. And then, Phillip, my brother, Regina, and Potyo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were they approximately in '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- my oldest sister was in her 20s and so was-- you know, when they took us away, you mean? Ranging from 20-some down to 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Potyo was 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: Potyo was 13 and a 1/2 when she was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were how old when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was over 20. I am over 70 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: You look wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened 50 years ago. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother Phillip?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Phillip was nearing military age, I suppose. Something like 18, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Regina?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Regina was 15, 16, some such.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Chica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chica was probably about 22, 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't hold me to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a year here or there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And there was somebody-- is that Yolan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yolan was Zippy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zippy. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister was in her 20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was the oldest of all of the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresa Harpert was her maiden name, H-A-R-P-E-R-T. And she came from [NON-ENGLISH], which was another Hungarian town where he was the daughter-- she was the daughter of the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: The chief rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The chief rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was-- he was the chief rabbi. I never met him. He died during the First World War. And his story, according to my mother, was that he simply became a heart patient from the heartache of the First World War. He couldn't bear men killing other human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father's name was Menyhert, M-E-N-Y-H-E-R-T, Katz, Menyhert Katz. And he came from a village just a few kilometers away from where I was born, and he was the son of landowners. My father was a great Talmudic scholar and a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a wine and liquor wholesale business.

speaker: interviewer
text: That he ran out of Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was doing some export import stuff. He was traveling a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow up in Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we grew up in Kisvarda. We went to school there and were taken to Auschwitz from Kisvarda.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like-- I want to talk about your growing up years. What was it like to grow up in Kisvarda? What kind of village was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a so-called city or town surrounded by little hamlets. And it was a rather sophisticated town. And the reason for that was probably the fact that there were a lot of prince and barons and in the surrounding villages. And they had their fancy affairs of horse racing and auto racing and prancing around on the main street of my town when they came shopping smelling of French perfumes and elegant clothes. And there were cafes, and there were mask balls, I believe, every year. I remember that. That they, they had-- they had a good life. And the Jews, some had a good life, but most of them were probably poor, I would imagine. I came from a middle-class family and had a nice home. And we had a chamber maid and, and wet nurse for the children. All of this was-- you know, some people had a better life, but most of them were poor, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the Jewish community in Kisvarda? Do you remember what it was like or how many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was a town of about 20,000. And there were several thousand Jews, maybe 4,000. It had to be 4,000 Jews rather than 4,000 families. But it was a large Jewish community. And it was a very-- I surely can recall a lot of Jewish life, an intelligent and motivated lifestyle where people studied and, and read and were interested in everything in the world, particularly in politics because somehow our lives always depended on the whims of the governments--

speaker: interviewer
text: Powers?

speaker: interviewee
text: --how anti-Semitic they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What kind of religious background did you come from? Was it an Orthodox home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come from a very Orthodox family. And though my father was one of those modern-looking Orthodox men, he was more Orthodox than any Jew who ever wore a shtreimel-- you know, the fur hat, which he did not-- and the kittel. And he had his clothes made, made to order, and his hat made to order. And he was a very beautiful man. And he was incredibly religious. And I understand, or I understood those days that he was highly regarded for his Talmudic knowledge and Talmudic intelligence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother cover her hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother wore a sheitel. But my mother was a highly cultivated, socially conscious human being who was very-- enormously well-read. And had it not been for the fact that she was a rabbi's daughter, she was-- her desire was to become a physician. And she was very, very enlightened and intelligent and incredibly aware of the world. And foretold us then-- god, so many decades ago-- that man will go to the moon. I grew up hearing those things. And she had friends-- she was working with my father in his business. And she had friends coming from all over Europe. And the six of us would stand around and take in eagerly all those big subjects and big words that were expressed by all of the people who came visiting, either on business or on social.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any books, the type of books that might have been in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was filled with leather-bound, beautiful, old Talmudic scholarly works-- beautiful, large, leather-bound works, many of them which were written by my ancestors. A lot of them, I understand. I couldn't read them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they in Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were spread out on Saturdays all over the tables. My father was studying from them. And the non-Hebraic works were books on loan from the library. And the library happened to be in our courtyard. And there were six children going once a week to the library to get books and one for my mother. And we took the allotted books. I don't know how many they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember that my mother devoured all of the books before we got through with the one we were supposed to be reading. And she was wanted to know, when are we going back to the library again?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was a household where there was constant talk about literature, theater. We had a maid who was a frustrated opera singer. And she-- you know, in my memory, she had a magnificent voice. And I sing along with Saturday broadcasts of the Metropolitan Opera, all of the operas that I heard her sing, you know, from to everything. And we loved her. Her name was Ida [INAUDIBLE]. And there was constant talk of books, and politics, and social conditions in the world, and the injustices. And one kid or another was always on top of the kitchen table reciting the latest poem that appeared in one of the magazines or newspapers. That was a very-- poetry was a very important thing in my day, in my country. And we were smitten by it. And somebody was always reciting poetry or imitating an actress from a movie. It was a very colorful household. And all of the friends that each of us had would flock to my home because it was such a special home. In the same religious household such as ours, the lifestyle was very different. And there were even more children in those homes than in ours. We had six, but they had sometimes 10, 12, or 14 children. And they were literally driven out of their homes, and they ran to ours to listen to poetry and talk about books. And, you know, it probably sounds kind of strange to talk in such a positive cultural way about a little town like Kisvarda. But my god, that's how it was. It really was in my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother had no problem taking all these children in? She was open?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was-- you know, somebody-- the main meal took place lunchtime. And if somebody walked in at lunchtime, my mother would just say, I'll just put more water in the soup. And the person automatically would be invited to stay for dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbat, Shabbos, the Saturday-- Friday night, what was it like at your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of cleaning for the Shabbat and a lot of baking of the challah. And, and all those-- what is it called that we eat on Saturday morning? The danishes, you know? Prune danish and-- you don't have it. But prune danish and cheese danish and all kinds of-- lots of stuff for six growing kids. Lots of stuff to be baked. And then always the smell of gefilte fish and chicken soup. And the cholent, the cholent which they took to the bakery to cook overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was beans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the breaking of the bread. Every week, they baked six for each day, one giant round bread, brown and white. And they would knead the dough and take them in straw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Baskets?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Baskets with a white towel in it that they sprinkled some flour. And they poured this kneaded dough into it. And out came-- some hours later, they brought home these magnificent breads. I think it was on Thursday that they baked the bread. And on Fridays, they baked all these things for the Shabbat and cooked the gefilte fish, and the turkey, and the chicken soup because we are not supposed to cook on a Saturday. You had to have what they called a Shabbos goy, who would light the light and put it out and who would go and get the cholent from the bakery. And, you know, certain things were not supposed to be done. It was sacrilegious to do certain work on, on Saturday. Well, that much for that. It was a lot of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Friday night, was there singing around the table?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was. And a lot of good family feelings. And my father, as a matter of fact-- if we were unruly and would fight as kids, would-- once we were finished with the dinner, my father would say, oh, they just have to eat and then they're fine. Then they're in great mood because we were eating like there was no tomorrow. We were six growing, healthy, beautiful, intelligent children and full of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your aspirations as a young child for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go out into the world. Leave the little town, and make something of myself. I was a superb student. That, again, sounds funny. But I was the best student in the class always. And go out into the world and have some great profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: At one point, I think reciting poetry and acting. And who knows? All kinds of things, like being a scientist or anything. We were just filled with life and desire. And always, I guess, had high ideas about life's promises. Which, of course, were all not so in the end. But we had very high desires. How did Hitler's rise to power in '33-- in 1933, did it affect your town at all? Did it affect Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the country I come from is a very special country in as much as that all of Hitler's doings did not reach us personally until the very end. And then it reached us with a greater bang, perhaps, than any other country because Hungary was an ally of Hitler. And he didn't have to march into Hungary to get a food-rich country like Hungary to rob the country of all its best food in order to feed his own people so there would be no uprising in Germany, I suppose. And we, we were relatively-- we felt relatively safe, even though anti-Semitism was our daily bread from the day I remember. I could never walk down the street without being called dirty Jew, dirty Jew, [NON-ENGLISH], a smelly Jew, by the very classmates I had. Partly, they were envious because I was a good student and because that was the name of the game, hate the Jews. I do not remember-- I never remember not feeling anti-Semitism. We thought that's the way it is all over the world. And certainly, that way in Kisvarda. And you simply have to endure it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you went to public schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to public school because I did not go to the Jewish school as most of the Jewish children went because my father was-- believed in the Messiah. And Israel was not based on the-- you know, the new Israel that came-- Palestine-- he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was anti-Zionist?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was anti-Zionist, so he wouldn't let us go to the Hebrew school. So we went to the public school. And he had a Hebrew teacher come and teach us how to pray from the Hebrew books.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you experienced anti-Semitism at school? In school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the teachers. From the students. Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an example of something that happened as a child that you would recall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly, walking down the street after school, walking down main street, just going for a stroll and bumping into the same kids I went to school with during the day. And calling me a dirty Jew, but very quietly so that I might-- you know, they knew I-- they wanted me to hear it, but they did it sort of discreetly, whispering between their teeth. And I was afraid of them. I was afraid of my teachers. There was corporal punishment. You know, they were hitting us. The teachers would hit us. And there was-- it was no fun-- not only not fun, but threatening. And but we never knew that-- you know, nobody imagined that anything like Auschwitz can happen. And because we were fairly safe in our homes in Hungary after-- you know, in the Hitler years. Not until Hitler actually marched into Hungary did everything happen with such incredible speed because by that time, the system was so highly developed that it was a push-button system. And the destruction of the Hungarian Jews was not only the swiftest, but Auschwitz, I understood then, from the kapo who spoke to me, that in all the years, the few people who survived for several years-- some Slovak girl. Her name was Rozsika. She was a very beautiful girl. And she perished. And she told me that in all the years that she survived in Auschwitz, she has never seen such carnage as when the Hungarian Jews were brought in. Instead of burning 10,000 people a day, it could be as many as from 10,000 to 24,000 a day. And the brutality and the carnage was at a higher-- you know, more highly developed than any other time previously.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to ask you one more question before our tape runs out. If you recall the day-- March 19, 1944, when Hitler marched into Budapest. Was there something that happened in Kisvarda on that day that was different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You probably know that I have written some books, and these stories are in my books. And one of the stories is of how on March 19, my youngest sister, Potyo, was sitting in a little room which we called the pink room. And on the table, there was a radio. There was no television in those days. And she was listening, and she knew all of the programs. And particularly, she knew all of the programs because she was already kicked out of school because she had an operation of-- what is it you have in  your--

speaker: interviewer
text: Appendix?

speaker: interviewee
text: Appendix. And she was out those days for six weeks from school. And they wouldn't take her back when she was ready to return. And the poor child was condemned to listen to the radio all the time because she couldn't go to school. And she was-- she was listening Sunday afternoon on March 19 to the radio. And suddenly she said, hey, everybody listen. There's something wrong. It's supposed to be some kind of happy music-- I don't remember what it was-- and they're playing some very tragic music, something that sounded like probably Beethoven or something. I don't remember what the piece was. And she had a very definite sense that something is wrong. And then it passed. There were all these friends around. It was Sunday afternoon. We were having a good time. And Monday morning on the 20th, very early in the morning, somebody knocked on the window and said, it's me, [? Shani, ?] or [? Lotzi, ?] whatever his Hungarian name was, one of the friends my brother had. And he went to school in Budapest. And he says, let me in. Let me in. And we let him in. And he told us this horrible story of the Nazis marched in Sunday afternoon-- which of course they did not announce on the radio right then-- when my sister sensed it. And he said that they came into the school, and people were starting to run through the windows and throwing chairs. And he managed in this madness, in this-- it was absolutely awful, he said. There was such fear, such terror everywhere. And he made his way to the railroad station. And he will-- he came to our house, and he will go back to his village to his parents-- which they lived a few miles away-- right after he's talking to us. And so he did. He told us this horrible Sunday that he lived through in Budapest. And he went back to his parents, and we never heard from him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But by then, Monday morning, it was quite obvious that they marched in. And their laws, the anti-Jewish laws, were printed in the papers and announced by the, the little man who was-- oh, how did they call it? God, help me. A town crier. Town crier. And he allowed-- every few hours, he brought new rules. And we would gather in the town square and listen to how we will have to wear a yellow star. And after 7 o'clock, you're not allowed out. We're finished with this segment of the tape. And I'd love to continue this particular part on our next tape. So thank you very much. We'll talk to you again soon.
